topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The campus map, once a comforting constellation of familiar landmarks, now seemed to mock me with its outdated markings. I traced the outline of the humanities building with a finger, the texture of the paper rough beneath my touch. Every corner felt suddenly hollow, the usual buzz of student life replaced by an unnerving silence. I kept glancing at the bulletin board near the main quad, half-expecting some hastily printed announcement to materialize, but there was only the usual clutter of flyers and forgotten notices. My shoulders slumped, a heavy, sinking feeling settling in my chest. "This can't be right," I muttered to the empty air, my voice sounding thin and brittle. I started walking again, my steps slow and deliberate, as if testing the very ground beneath my feet to see if it was solid anymore.

[story 2]

I sat at my desk in the library, the afternoon sun slanting through the dusty window, illuminating motes of dancing debris in the air. My laptop screen reflected back my own face—pale, unreadable. I had reread the administrative memos three times, each reading confirming the same bleak reality: the institution was winding down. I picked up my pen, intending to start an essay, but the ink just seemed to smear across the page. I tapped the pen repeatedly against the desk, a nervous rhythm that escalated into a frantic tapping. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small space between the shelves, running a hand through my already messy hair. "What am I supposed to *do* now?" I whispered, the question hanging heavy and unanswered in the quiet room.

[story 3]

The final meeting was a blur of hushed voices and shuffling papers. I stood near the exit doors, feeling a strange detachment, as if watching a play where I was no longer an actor. When the official notice was finally read aloud, the sound seemed to distort, stretching into something unrecognizable. My breath hitched, a sharp, involuntary intake of air that felt like swallowing broken glass. I didn't move for a long moment; my feet remained rooted to the linoleum floor. Then, I turned slowly, looking around the cavernous hall as if searching for an escape route that simply didn't exist. A coldness seeped into my bones, a profound sense of disorientation washing over me. I finally managed to nod, a small, jerky movement, before turning and walking toward the exit, my gaze fixed firmly on the ground ahead of me.
